# 🇱🇰 Sri Lanka This Week

![LastUpdated](https://img.shields.io/badge/last_updated-2026--03--20_06:24:41-green)
[![RSS](https://img.shields.io/badge/RSS-Feed-orange)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nuuuwan/lk_news_digest/refs/heads/main/rss.xml)

*Generated by gpt-5 from 202 English News Articles published between 2026-03-13 and 2026-03-20.*

## 1. A Gulf war stress-tests Sri Lanka’s recovery as Colombo reverts to fuel rationing

The escalation between the US–Israel and Iran has exposed how little slack exists in Sri Lanka’s energy system. With oil above $100, insurers balking and shipping calendars in flux, the Government reintroduced the QR-based National Fuel Pass and trimmed midweek activity—schools moved to a four-day week and state offices to conservation rosters. The Ceylon Chamber urged a clear list of priority sectors, flexible work, transparent fuel pricing and a backstop for aviation bunkers; the Government promised special allocations for farmers and hospitals, and gave dollar-earners temporary leave to import fuel for the tourism and export trades. Buffers built since 2022 (a working pricing formula, some reserves, lower inflation) help, but ad hoc measures will not. CPC must publish its price build-up, SOE reforms must accelerate, and contingency contracts—not press notices—should underpin LPG and crude supplies. Otherwise, panic buying defeats policy and black markets return.

## 2. Markets blink as oil surges, but the macro repair must hold

Oil’s climb and war-risk premiums knocked equities—ASPI shed over 2,000 points in five sessions—though the rupee held and government bonds barely budged. Yet real activity is not collapsing: February PMIs for manufacturing (56.8) and services (54.4) still signal expansion, and factory output rose in January. A puzzle remains: labour-force participation has slipped to the high-40s while plants are busier, suggesting migration, informality or skills mismatch. Policy should resist blanket subsidies and import controls; keep the IMF programme on track; tilt support to working capital for exporters, targeted cash transfers for the poor, and time-bound measures (e.g., school/office rostering) that save fuel without killing demand. 🧭 Knee‑jerk price caps today become shortages tomorrow.

## 3. Energy management needs rules, not rallies

The Government has ordered ministries to file data-driven conservation plans and tasked the CPC to coordinate sectoral allocations. That is progress. But the weekend botch of the Fuel Pass relaunch—glitchy portals, absent FAQs, and a non‑compliant privacy policy under Sri Lanka’s Personal Data Protection Act—betrayed thin state capacity. If the public must sacrifice, the state must match it with competence: publish fuel stock dashboards; fix PDPA compliance (purpose, retention, rights and a named Data Protection Officer); and empower a single operations room to align ports, grid, health, fisheries and logistics. In crises, information is fuel.

## 4. Governance on trial: security, speech and investor confidence

The arrest of former SIS chief Suresh Sallay over alleged Easter‑Sunday links—and his family’s complaints over custodial conditions—alongside the detention of Lanka e News editor Sandaruwan Senadheera, have sharpened concerns over due process. In parallel, a new Protection of the State from Terrorism Bill (PSTA) has drawn fire from civil society for sweeping powers (Executive detention overrides, broad ‘terrorist publication’ clauses and curbs that could criminalise protest). Sri Lanka’s rule‑of‑law deficit is not a civil‑liberties footnote; it is a credit spread. Contracts and capital avoid jurisdictions where laws are broad and enforcement arbitrary. If Colombo wants the Port City to hum and FDIs to rise beyond $1bn, it must temper security with safeguards, ensure humane custody, and legislate narrowly, with independent oversight.

## 5. From ration coupons to reform: fix the state you have

President Anura Kumara Dissanayake reviewed reforms across 124 non‑commercial agencies and pledged to keep the economy running through the shock. Trimming ceremonies is not enough. The 2026 tax debate revealed how the Inland Revenue’s incentives, appeal lags and IT errors morph compliance into coercion; transfer pricing enforcement lacks a domestic benchmarking database; and investors still pinball across ministries for approvals. Quick wins exist: create a true one‑stop FDI window with statutory teeth; publish an IRD service charter tied to measured timelines; build a comparable‑companies database with the Central Bank and CSE; and professionalise boards of SOEs. Reform is not a press conference; it is queues that disappear for the right reasons.

## 6. A humane opening with India’s refugees and a cooler political temperature

Colombo struck a welcome note of pragmatism. Leader of the House Bimal Rathnayake invited Sri Lankan Tamils in Tamil Nadu—c. 89,000 people—to return if they wish, backed UNHCR facilitation and non‑custodial entry; he also recognised that many, born and integrated in India, may lawfully choose Indian citizenship. Doing both—clearing paperwork hurdles at home while urging New Delhi to resolve limbo—reduces grievance without stoking numbers. It also signals to southern India that Sri Lanka’s left‑led Government can pair principle with administrative sense—a useful asset as Colombo courts South Asian tourists and trade.

## 7. Build resilience when the sky is blue: lessons from Ditwah to diesel

Four months after Cyclone Ditwah, too many displaced families still wait on land titles, documentation and livelihood support; relief arrived, recovery lagged. The same pattern risks repeating in energy. A Cabinet‑approved emergency coal tender stalled because of an underperforming supplier’s live contract; LPG procurement lacked built‑in contingencies and geographic diversification, even though a 24‑hour warning can cut disaster losses by 30% (UNDRR). Fix the plumbing now: maintain strategic stocks for critical fuels; embed “Plan B” suppliers and alternative load ports in tenders; cultivate native vegetation on landslide scars via hydroseeding and drone seeding; and move faster on EV two‑wheelers and cleaner marine fuel near Colombo’s sealanes. Grants such as India’s Rs. 264.6m for e‑motorcycles to fisheries officers are a start, not a strategy.

## 8. Trade, tourism and the Indian Ocean chessboard

War‑risk flight reroutes via Gulf hubs threaten visitor flows; tea shipments to the Middle East face pricier freight and insurance; and remittances—from which Sri Lanka earned c. $8bn in 2025—are exposed if Gulf labour markets wobble. The Export Development Board has convened sector groups to triage bottlenecks; that should feed into a single Cabinet‑level export taskforce with authority over ports, customs and shipping agents. Medium‑term, diversify routes (e.g., Turkish/Asean connectors), markets (India, East Asia, Africa), and services (IT, logistics and finance). And keep Colombo an honest broker at sea: Sri Lanka’s rescue of Iranian sailors after the sinking of IRIS Dena upheld maritime law and neutrality; that credibility is worth more than a slogan in a fragmented ‘Indo‑Pacific’.

## 9. Sustainability is not a luxury; it’s an energy hedge

When oil spikes, green talk gets tested. Yet corporates are leaning in: NDB raised LKR 16bn in GSS+ Tier II debt, ComBank and the Ceylon Chamber topped sustainability reporting awards, and INSEE’s Circularity Summit pressed resource efficiency. Small signals matter too: Colombo explores clean‑fuel rules for ships to cut PM2.5; Britol and Antler now blend certified recycled plastics into FMCG packaging; and a grant‑backed accelerator is turning water hyacinth from a menace into livelihoods. The state should match this with stick and carrot: uphold market‑reflective energy pricing (with targeted cash aid), fast‑track rooftop solar permits, and publish a time‑bound plan to cut public‑sector fossil use by a third. 🌱 The cheapest imported barrel is the one you do not need.

## Model Prompt (for [gpt-5](https://platform.openai.com/docs/models/gpt-5))

```
You will be given several Sri Lankan news articles from the past week. Write a concise, cohesive editorial digest in the style of The Economist, but output it as a JSON list. Each list element must be a dict of the form:

{
  "title": "...",
  "body": "..."
}

Titles should be written as crisp, fully informative sentences. Follow each title with a short paragraph in "body" that adds context and nuance without bullets.

Go beyond summary and analyse how the week’s developments fit together. Use emojis purposefully, and explain any non-obvious acronyms or jargon. Stick strictly to verifiable facts.
```
